title: Deploying AI to Production
description: Learn to take Claude-powered features from prototype to production — covering reliability, monitoring, cost optimization, error handling, and scaling strategies.
learningObjectives: Evaluate production readiness of an AI feature using a systematic checklist covering reliability, observability, and failure handling
Implement robust error handling, retry strategies, and fallback patterns for Claude API integrations
Design cost-effective, scalable AI architectures using caching, model routing, and monitoring best practices

sections.title: Production Readiness Checklist
sections.content:
Shipping an AI prototype that works in development is easy. Shipping an AI feature that works reliably for thousands of users under real-world conditions is a different challenge entirely. The gap between "it works on my machine" and "it works in production" is wider for AI features than for traditional software.

**Why AI features need a different production checklist.** Traditional features are deterministic — given the same input, they produce the same output. Claude-powered features are probabilistic. The same prompt can produce slightly different outputs each time, latency varies with input length, and failure modes include not just HTTP errors but also degraded output quality. Your production checklist needs to account for all of this.

**The Production Readiness Checklist**

Before any Claude-powered feature goes live, you should validate six critical areas. Each one addresses a different dimension of production risk, and skipping any of them is an invitation for a painful incident.

**Reliability** is where every production readiness review should begin. Think carefully about what happens when the Claude API is slow or unavailable. You need timeouts configured for every call, a fallback experience that keeps the application usable, and a graceful degradation strategy so that a single API hiccup doesn't cascade into a broken user experience.

**Quality assurance** for AI features goes well beyond traditional testing. You need an eval suite that runs against your prompts on a regular basis, coverage for adversarial inputs and edge cases, and testing across different languages and input formats. Crucially, you should verify that output quality holds up at the scale you expect — a prompt that works beautifully for ten requests might produce inconsistent results across ten thousand.

**Security** requires a layered approach. Sanitize user inputs before including them in prompts to guard against prompt injection attacks. Implement guardrails that detect and block injection attempts. Filter Claude's outputs to ensure that content which shouldn't reach your users — whether harmful, off-topic, or revealing of system internals — is caught before it's displayed.

**Data handling** is often overlooked until a compliance audit surfaces it. You need logging that captures enough detail for debugging without storing sensitive user data. Verify compliance with your data retention policies, and ensure that PII and confidential information are stripped from payloads before they're sent to the API.

**Cost projection** prevents budget surprises. Estimate API costs at your expected traffic volume, configure budget alerts to catch runaway spending, and optimize prompt length and model selection so you're not paying premium prices for tasks that a lighter model handles just as well.

Finally, **observability** is what lets you sleep at night. Can you track latency, error rates, token usage, and output quality in real time? Will you know if something breaks before your users report it? If your monitoring can't answer these questions, you have a blind spot that will eventually hurt you.

**The "Day 2" question.** Before you ship, ask yourself: When this feature breaks at 2 AM on a Saturday, will your on-call engineer be able to diagnose the problem from the logs and dashboards alone? If the answer is no, you're not production-ready.

adaptedExamples.developer: Before shipping your Claude-powered summarization endpoint, validate all six checklist areas: configure a 15-second timeout with a cached-response fallback, build an eval suite that tests adversarial inputs, sanitize user text against prompt injection, strip PII before API calls, estimate costs at projected traffic, and set up latency and error-rate dashboards so your on-call engineer can diagnose issues at 2 AM.

sections.title: Error Handling and Retry Strategies

playground.description: Practice designing error handling for a production API integration. You'll receive a realistic failure scenario and need to write a robust handler.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a senior backend engineer helping a colleague design error handling for a Claude API integration in a production Node.js/TypeScript service. When the user describes a failure scenario, respond with: (1) the specific error handling code they should write, using exponential backoff with jitter for retryable errors, (2) what fallback behavior to implement, and (3) what to log for incident investigation. Keep code examples concise and production-ready. Use TypeScript. Point out any gaps in their approach.
playground.userMessage: Our support ticket summarization endpoint is failing intermittently in production. We're seeing a mix of 429 (rate limit) and 503 (service unavailable) errors, about 5% of requests. Right now we just catch the error and return a 500 to the client. Help me write proper error handling that retries the right errors, falls back gracefully, and gives us enough logging to debug issues.

adaptedExamples.developer: When your support-ticket summarizer hits a 429 rate-limit error, retry with exponential backoff and jitter — for example, 1s, then ~2.5s, then ~6s — to avoid a thundering herd. For a sustained outage, fall back to showing the ticket's first three sentences as a plain-text summary rather than leaving the agent staring at a spinner that never resolves.

sections.title: Monitoring and Observability
sections.content:
You can't improve what you can't measure, and you can't fix what you can't see. Monitoring AI features requires tracking metrics that don't exist in traditional applications — token usage, output quality, prompt performance — alongside the usual suspects like latency and error rates.

**The four pillars of AI observability**

Effective AI observability rests on four pillars, each answering a different fundamental question about your system's health.

The first pillar is **operational metrics**, which tell you whether the system is working. These are the numbers your on-call engineer checks first during an incident:

- Request latency (p50, p95, p99) broken down by feature and model
- Error rate by type — rate limits, timeouts, and server errors each tell a different story
- Requests per minute, so you can anticipate rate limit issues before they hit
- Queue depth, if you're using async processing

The second pillar is **cost metrics**, which tell you whether the system is affordable. Track input and output tokens per request at both average and p95 levels, since a few outlier requests can skew your costs dramatically. Roll these up into cost per request, cost per feature, and cost per user, and chart daily and monthly spend with trend lines so you can spot cost creep before it becomes a budget crisis.

The third pillar is **quality metrics**, which tell you whether the system is actually useful to your users. Output acceptance rate — whether the user used what Claude produced — is your single most important quality signal. Complement it with regeneration rate (how often users ask Claude to try again), edit distance (how much users modify Claude's output before using it), and eval suite scores tracked over time to catch regressions.

The fourth pillar is **safety metrics**, which tell you whether the system is behaving responsibly. Track these signals and investigate any sudden spikes, as they may indicate an attack, a prompt regression, or a new user behavior pattern that your guardrails weren't designed for:

- Content filter trigger rates
- Prompt injection detection frequency
- Out-of-scope request volume
- User-reported quality issues

**What to log for every request**

Create a structured log entry for every Claude API call. The fields below capture enough information for debugging and analysis without risking PII exposure:

```typescript
interface AIRequestLog {
  requestId: string;
  timestamp: string;
  feature: string;          // which product feature triggered this
  model: string;            // claude-sonnet-4-20250514, etc.
  inputTokens: number;
  outputTokens: number;
  latencyMs: number;
  statusCode: number;
  userId: string;           // hashed, not PII
  promptVersion: string;    // track which prompt version was used
  // DO NOT log the full prompt or response in production
  // if they may contain user PII
}
```

**Building actionable dashboards**

A wall of metrics is useless if you can't act on them. The most effective dashboards are organized around the questions your team actually needs to answer.

The first question is **"Is it working right now?"** — answer it with real-time error rate, latency, and active request count panels. Set alerts for error rates above 5% or p95 latency exceeding your timeout threshold. The second question is **"Is it getting worse?"** — answer it with week-over-week trends for quality metrics, cost per request, and latency. A gradual increase in regeneration rate, for example, might signal prompt degradation or a model update that subtly changed output behavior.

The third question is **"Where is the money going?"** — answer it with a cost breakdown by feature, model, and time period. This view helps you identify which features are disproportionately expensive and whether cost scales linearly with user growth. The fourth question is **"Is it safe?"** — answer it by charting content filter triggers, prompt injection attempts, and out-of-scope requests over time.

**Alerting strategy**

Not every metric needs an alert. Focus on signals that demand action and set thresholds that match the urgency of the response required.

- **Page immediately:** Error rate above 20% for 5+ minutes, all requests failing, or authentication errors indicating a revoked API key
- **Alert during business hours:** Error rate above 5% for 15+ minutes, p95 latency doubling from baseline, or cost anomaly exceeding 2x the daily average
- **Review weekly:** Quality metric trends, per-feature cost trends, and emerging patterns in safety metrics

adaptedExamples.developer: Log a structured entry for every Claude API call that captures request ID, feature name, model, input/output tokens, latency, status code, hashed user ID, and prompt version — but never the raw prompt or response if they may contain PII. Build dashboards organized around four questions: Is it working now? Is it getting worse? Where is the money going? Is it safe?

sections.title: Cost Optimization

playground.description: Optimize a real prompt for cost efficiency. You'll work through reducing token usage while maintaining output quality.
playground.systemPrompt: You are an AI cost optimization consultant helping a developer reduce their Claude API spend. When the user shares a prompt and usage context, analyze the token efficiency and provide specific, actionable recommendations: (1) rewrite the system prompt to be shorter without losing critical instructions, (2) recommend the right model tier for the task, (3) suggest caching strategies, and (4) estimate the cost savings as a percentage. Show before/after token counts where possible. Be concrete — don't just say 'make it shorter', show exactly how.
playground.userMessage: We're spending $2,400/month on our Claude API for a feature that classifies incoming customer emails into 6 categories and extracts the customer's order number and sentiment. We process about 15,000 emails per day. Our current system prompt is 800 tokens long with detailed examples for each category, and we're using claude-sonnet-4-20250514 for everything. Average input is 1,200 tokens per email (system prompt + email body) and we set max_tokens to 1024 even though the classification response is typically under 100 tokens. Help me cut costs.

adaptedExamples.developer: Route your email-classification endpoint to Haiku instead of Sonnet — it handles six-category classification just as accurately at a fraction of the cost. Set max_tokens to 100 instead of 1024 since the response is under 50 tokens, trim your 800-token system prompt to essentials, and cache identical classifications with a one-hour TTL. These changes alone can cut your $2,400/month API spend by over 60%.

sections.title: Scaling Considerations

adaptedExamples.developer: Implement a priority queue that serves interactive user requests before background batch jobs, so a bulk document-analysis run doesn't starve real-time features of their rate-limit budget. Add a circuit breaker that trips after five consecutive API failures, returning cached or degraded responses while the service recovers rather than hammering an unhealthy endpoint with retries.

sections.title: Design a Production Deployment Plan
sections.content: Apply everything you've learned to design a production deployment plan for a real AI feature.

exercise.prompt:
You're deploying a Claude-powered feature for an internal customer support tool. The feature reads incoming support tickets, classifies them by urgency (critical, high, normal, low) and department (billing, technical, account, general), extracts key entities (customer ID, order number, product name), and generates a suggested first response for the support agent.

The system handles approximately 500 tickets per hour during peak times and 50 per hour during off-peak. Support agents work in shifts across three time zones.

Design a complete production deployment plan covering:

1. **Model selection** — Which Claude model(s) would you use for each sub-task (classification, extraction, response generation)? Justify your choices based on the capability-cost tradeoff.

2. **Error handling strategy** — How do you handle API failures for each sub-task? What are your fallback experiences? What timeouts do you set?

3. **Cost estimation and optimization** — Estimate the approximate token usage and daily cost. What caching or optimization strategies would you apply?

4. **Monitoring plan** — What are the 5 most important metrics you'd track? What thresholds would trigger alerts?

5. **Scaling plan** — How do you handle the 10x difference between peak and off-peak traffic? What happens if ticket volume doubles unexpectedly?
hints: Consider using different models for different sub-tasks — classification and extraction might not need the same model as response generation
For fallback experiences, think about what the support agent's workflow looked like BEFORE this AI feature existed — that's your natural degraded mode
When estimating costs, remember that the suggested response generation will use far more output tokens than classification or extraction
For the scaling plan, consider whether all sub-tasks need to scale equally — classification might be more critical than response generation during a spike
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should demonstrate: (1) Thoughtful model selection — using a lighter model (Haiku) for classification and extraction where structured outputs are well-defined, and a more capable model (Sonnet) for response generation where quality matters more. Justifications should reference speed, cost, and capability tradeoffs. (2) Layered error handling — different strategies for each sub-task. Classification failures might fall back to keyword-based rules. Extraction failures mean the agent sees the raw ticket. Response generation failures mean no suggested response, but the agent can still work. Timeouts should be shorter for classification (3-5s) and longer for response generation (15-30s). (3) A reasonable cost estimate showing token usage breakdown by sub-task, with optimization strategies including caching for similar tickets, prompt compression, and Anthropic's prompt caching for the system prompt. Should identify response generation as the primary cost driver. (4) Specific, actionable metrics — not generic 'latency and errors' but metrics tied to the feature: classification accuracy (measured by agent overrides), response acceptance rate (measured by agent edits), cost per ticket, p95 latency by sub-task, and API error rate. Alert thresholds should be concrete numbers. (5) A scaling plan that handles the peak/off-peak variance — possibly with a request queue, rate limit awareness, and a strategy for unexpected volume spikes such as processing classification first and queuing response generation.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Think through a real-world production incident scenario.

exercise.prompt:
It's Monday morning and your AI-powered feature — which generates personalized product recommendations for an e-commerce site — is showing two concerning signals:

1. The p95 latency has increased from 2 seconds to 8 seconds over the past hour.
2. Your cost dashboard shows that yesterday's API spend was 3x the daily average, even though traffic was normal.

Walk through your investigation process for each issue. What would you check first? What are the most likely root causes? What immediate actions would you take while investigating? How would you prevent each issue from recurring?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should demonstrate systematic debugging thinking. For the latency issue: (1) Check if it's API-side (Anthropic status page, latency measured before and after the API call) or application-side (queue depth, database queries, network). (2) Likely root causes include: increased input token count (maybe a prompt change went out that added more context), model change in configuration, increased traffic being queued, or API-side degradation. (3) Immediate actions: check for recent deployments/prompt changes, check rate limit headers on responses, consider enabling cached fallbacks or switching to a faster model temporarily. For the cost spike: (1) Check token usage breakdown by feature and by input/output. (2) Likely root causes: a prompt change that increased system prompt size, a bug causing retries or duplicate requests, a missing max_tokens cap on a feature, or a code change that sends more context than intended. (3) Immediate actions: identify which feature's cost increased, check deployment logs for recent changes, add a cost cap/alert. Prevention: prompt change review process, automated cost anomaly detection, max_tokens enforcement, canary deployments that compare cost metrics.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Taking AI features from prototype to production requires intentional engineering across five dimensions.

**Reliability comes first.** Every Claude API call can fail, and your system must handle that gracefully. Implement retries with exponential backoff for transient errors, set appropriate timeouts tailored to each feature's UX requirements, and design fallback experiences that keep your application useful even when the API is unavailable. The goal is not to prevent failures — they're inevitable — but to make failures invisible to your users.

**Monitor what matters.** Effective observability rests on four pillars: operational metrics like latency and error rates, cost metrics like token usage and spend, quality metrics like acceptance rate and edit distance, and safety metrics like filter triggers and injection attempts. Build dashboards organized around the questions your on-call engineer needs to answer at 2 AM, and set alert thresholds that distinguish between "investigate tomorrow" and "wake someone up now."

**Optimize costs deliberately.** Model selection is the highest-leverage cost decision you'll make. Use Haiku for simple, well-defined tasks, Sonnet for the majority of production features, and Opus only where the quality premium is justified by the use case. Beyond model selection, reduce input tokens by trimming context aggressively, cache responses to avoid redundant API calls, and leverage Anthropic's prompt caching for stable system prompts that repeat across requests.

**Plan for scale from day one.** Understand your rate limits on both RPM and TPM dimensions, because hitting either one will throttle your application. Use request queuing with priority levels to ensure interactive traffic is served before background work. Move heavy or long-running workloads to async architectures that decouple user experience from API latency. Build circuit breakers that protect both your application and the API during outages.

**Test before you ship.** Run your eval suite to verify output quality, simulate API failures to validate your error handling, project costs at expected traffic volumes to avoid budget surprises, and confirm that your monitoring and alerting will surface problems before your users report them. Production readiness isn't a single gate — it's an ongoing discipline.
